headline: California's Green Hydrogen Law Plugs a Renewable Energy Gap
meta_description: Governor Newsom signed SB 1350, making green hydrogen eligible for renewable energy credits and addressing California's nighttime power shortfall.
summary_short: California just solved a 40-year clean energy problem. Governor Newsom signed a law recognizing green hydrogen as renewable, unlocking financial incentives for facilitie…
body:
California produces 60 percent of its electricity from renewables—an impressive number that masks an awkward truth. That other 40 percent arrives precisely when solar fades and wind reserves deplete: overnight, in winter, when fossil fuels step in to fill the dark.

Green hydrogen offers a workaround. By using renewable energy to split water into hydrogen and oxygen, then storing and converting it back through fuel cells when needed, solar power generated at noon can power homes at 2 a.m. in January.

Governor Newsom recently signed SB 1350 into law, officially qualifying green hydrogen electricity as renewable under the state's Renewables Portfolio Standard. Facilities meeting three requirements—sourcing hydrogen from state-certified renewables, maintaining at least 20 percent green hydrogen in their fuel blend, and reducing air pollution—now earn valuable financial credits.

The bill passed unanimously. Already, the Lodi Energy Center, 15 miles north of Stockton, plans to run on green hydrogen. Element Resources' Lancaster Clean Energy Center, opening in 2028, will become the nation's largest green hydrogen facility.

For utilities and developers, the law signals something crucial: confidence. California isn't just talking about bridging its energy gap anymore. It's building the infrastructure to do it.
